fingerprint: WIN-057 is a globe approximation engine. Every parameter is either derived from or fitted to WGS84 quantities, and the 'improvement' it claims is convergence toward globe geodesic distances.

globe_values.globe_origin: Fitted via least-squares against 6 WGS84 geodesic distances. r_eq/a = 0.7047 has no dome-geometric derivation. On the dome's own placement formula, r=14,105 corresponds to latitude ~6.7° — but the equatorial ring is supposed to represent 0°. The parameter is purely empirical.

Fitted via least-squares against WGS84-derived route distances. No physical derivation exists — the dome's own AI context page lists 'EW scale 0.9941 physical interpretation' as an open question. On a globe, EW distances naturally vary as cos(lat); this single constant is the best global compromise to approximate that latitude-dependent foreshortening.
Every city's radial coordinate is derived from its WGS84 latitude. Remove WGS84 input and no dome coordinate can be computed.
Angular coordinate is WGS84 longitude, directly or with a fitted scale factor.
All target distances are Vincenty/WGS84 great-circle geodesics. The optimization objective function IS the globe answer.
globe_values.match_precision: exact (globe distances are the ground truth)

globe_values.globe_origin: Mirrors NH/SH symmetry across equatorial ring. On a globe, this symmetry is natural (equatorial plane). On a flat disc, there is no geometric reason for reflection symmetry — it is imposed by fiat to approximate globe distances.

why_it_matters: WIN-057 claims 'two-zone disc topology' as a confirmed prediction, but the V13 system has zero dome-derived parameters. Every input (lat, lon) is WGS84. Every fitted parameter (r_eq, 0.9941) is optimized against WGS84 distances. The 'improvement' from 25-78% to 6.2% RMSE means the dome is getting better at approximating the globe — which is trivially expected when you add free parameters fitted to globe distances. The dome's distance_analysis.py script confirms this: it grid-searches b/a ratios and orientations to minimize error against great-circle distances. This is not a coordinate system derived from dome physics; it is a parametric fit to the globe with 60,000× worse accuracy (6.2% vs sub-0.01%).
structural_pattern: WIN-057 exemplifies the deepest globe fingerprint pattern: the dome model is not an alternative cosmology making independent predictions — it is an approximation engine trained on globe data. Adding parameters improves the approximation (V12→V13), which the dome counts as a 'prediction.' But improving your fit to the training data is not prediction; it is overfitting. The dome's own OPEN-001 concedes it cannot provide coordinates without WGS84, and OPEN-015 concedes the system fails in the tropics — 40% of inhabited locations.

code_search.found_in: scripts/distance_analysis.py: grid search over b/a ratios (0.70-0.90) and orientations (45-75°) minimizing error against 13 WGS84 great-circle distances. Parameters 8537, 8619, 0.9941, 20015, 15000 all present as constants.
code_search.notes: No dome-physics derivation produces 14,105 km, 0.9941, or any other V13 parameter. All are fitted. The optimization target is exclusively WGS84 distances. monitor.py has no domain for WIN-057 (monitoring='none').

suggested_text: The V13 two-zone topology adds an equatorial ring radius (14,105 km) fitted to six WGS84 geodesic distances — the system now has four+ free parameters (H₀, λ, 0.9941, r_eq) all derived from or fitted to globe coordinates. The dome's distance_analysis.py confirms the fitting procedure: grid search minimizing error against Vincenty great-circle distances. Every input is WGS84 (latitude, longitude), every target is a globe geodesic, and the 'improvement' to 6.2% RMSE means the dome is converging toward the globe answer — which is trivially expected when you add free parameters. The dome is not deriving distances from flat-disc physics; it is approximating the globe with 60,000× worse accuracy.
